questions.question: Why is a workflow engine usually more than just a queue plus workers?
options: Because it must preserve durable state, timers, and progress across long-running execution.
Because queues cannot carry payloads larger than one kilobyte.
Because workflow engines never call external services.
Because workers in a queue system cannot fail.
questions.explanation: Durable orchestration state is the main extra requirement.

questions.question: What is the main reason activity idempotency matters in workflow systems?
options: Activities may be retried after ambiguous or partial failure.
It makes timers unnecessary.
It guarantees total ordering across workflows.
It removes the need for workflow history.
questions.explanation: Retries are standard behavior in long-running orchestration.

questions.question: Which signal best reveals that the timer subsystem is becoming a bottleneck?
options: Overdue timer count and wakeup lag.
Average workflow name length.
The number of dashboard colors in the operator UI.
Median source-file size of worker binaries.
questions.explanation: Timer lag exposes whether delayed work is being resumed on time.

questions.question: Why is workflow versioning a first-class platform concern?
options: Because running workflows can outlive deploys and need compatible logic over time.
Because versioning replaces the need for retries.
Because it allows activities to skip persistence.
Because workflows cannot be monitored without version numbers.
questions.explanation: Long-lived executions and code evolution interact directly.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with keeping a durable workflow event history?
options: It improves recovery and auditability but increases storage and replay overhead.
It guarantees external systems cannot fail.
It removes the need for operator tools.
It makes compensation logic unnecessary.
questions.explanation: Strong recovery comes with platform cost.

questions.question: Which failure is most directly mitigated by activity heartbeats?
options: A long-running worker becoming stuck or dying mid-step.
A topic being deleted accidentally.
A metrics query returning stale data.
A queue partition becoming hot.
questions.explanation: Heartbeats help detect abandoned or stalled activity work.

questions.question: Why are compensation hooks often better than pretending rollback is automatic?
options: Because external side effects may need explicit cleanup steps after partial success.
Because compensation eliminates all consistency trade-offs.
Because rollback is forbidden in distributed systems.
Because compensation only matters for user-interface workflows.
questions.explanation: Real systems coordinate side effects that are not transactionally reversible.

questions.question: What design change most improves tenant or workflow-family isolation in a shared engine?
options: Per-type quotas and reserved worker capacity.
A single global queue for all activity types.
Removing operator cancellation tools.
Disabling retries platform-wide.
questions.explanation: Shared orchestration systems need explicit isolation controls.
